evals.prompt: Build a PrestaShop 9 module settings page for an external newsletter service. It needs an API key field, a remote-group selector, save/load logic, and a generated cron URL for sync.
evals.expected_output: The response designs the module using real PrestaShop 9 architecture instead of improvising fake framework layers. It uses the modern admin configuration approach, keeps the main module class thin, and handles sync/cron concerns concretely.
expectations: It uses a modern Symfony-based admin configuration flow rather than stuffing large HTML into `getContent()`.
It keeps the main module class thin and focused on registration/bootstrap concerns.
It treats the cron endpoint as machine-facing and protected rather than an admin-page side effect.
It avoids inventing PrestaShop framework classes or namespaces.

evals.prompt: Review a PrestaShop module that syncs newsletter subscribers to a remote API and exposes a cron endpoint. I want architectural defects, data-model mistakes, and security problems called out plainly.
evals.expected_output: The response performs a real PrestaShop-aware review rather than a generic PHP review. It identifies architectural, persistence, and cron/security defects that matter for a PrestaShop 9 module.
expectations: It calls out whether remote IDs are modeled explicitly instead of assuming email is always enough.
It treats cron security and authentication seriously.
It distinguishes front-office legacy controllers from modern back-office admin controllers correctly.
It avoids praising installation success as proof that the module architecture is sound.

evals.prompt: Fix a PrestaShop 9 module that installs, but its config page routing is broken and the form save/load flow does not behave like a modern PS9 admin page.
evals.expected_output: The response fixes the broken architecture in PrestaShop terms. It routes the config page through the right modern admin path and restores a save/load flow that matches current PrestaShop expectations.
expectations: It routes the configuration page through a modern Symfony admin flow.
It restores a coherent save/load cycle using real PrestaShop patterns.
It does not fall back to a giant inline legacy page render as the main fix.
It stays grounded in PrestaShop 9 rather than generic Symfony module advice.
